notes: 52-week rotation. Each week shows historical chess events that happened in that calendar week across history. Pick: weekOfYear (ET) mod 52.

events.text: Emanuel Lasker, World Champion for 27 years (1894–1921), dies in New York on January 11 at age 72.
Lasker and Carl Schlechter begin their World Championship match on January 7 — a tied 10-game contest that left Lasker on the throne.
Wilhelm Steinitz and Johannes Zukertort begin the first official World Championship match on January 11 in New York.
Bobby Fischer, the 11th World Champion, dies in Reykjavik on January 17 at age 64 — the same number as the squares on the board he made his life's work.
Mikhail Chigorin, the father of the Russian school of chess, dies in Lublin on January 25 at age 57.
Garry Kasparov plays his ‘Pearl of Wijk’ against Veselin Topalov on January 20 — a 44-move queen sacrifice still considered one of the most beautiful games ever played.
Steinitz and Mikhail Chigorin begin their first World Championship match in Havana on January 20 — Steinitz wins 10–6 with 1 draw.
Lasker defeats Frank Marshall in the World Championship match that ends January 26 — a crushing 8–0 with 7 draws.
FIDE president Florencio Campomanes terminates the unfinished Karpov–Kasparov match on February 15 after 48 games — the longest WC match in history at the time.
Garry Kasparov begins his first match against Deep Blue in Philadelphia on February 10 — the human champion wins 4–2.
Bobby Fischer’s Pasadena seclusion deepens — he refuses to defend his title and remains in self-exile.
José Raúl Capablanca, the 3rd World Champion, dies of a stroke in New York on March 8 at age 53 — ‘the human chess machine’ silenced.
Carl Schlechter’s legacy revisited as Lasker’s 1910 challenger is remembered for one of the closest WC matches ever drawn.
Mikhail Botvinnik and Vasily Smyslov complete their rematch on March 4 — Botvinnik regains his title 12½–10½, demonstrating his ‘rematch clause’ mastery.
Smyslov dethrones Botvinnik on March 4, becoming the 7th World Champion.
Robert James ‘Bobby’ Fischer is born in Chicago on March 9 — the prodigy who would conquer the chess world by age 29.
Alexander Alekhine, the 4th World Champion, dies alone in his Estoril hotel room on March 24 — the only champion to die while holding the title.
Emanuel Lasker dethrones Wilhelm Steinitz, becoming the 2nd World Champion on March 15.
José Raúl Capablanca takes the World Championship from Lasker in Havana on March 15 — the Cuban champion never loses a single game in the match.
Botvinnik and David Bronstein begin their WC match on March 15 — drawn 12–12, Botvinnik retains via the tiebreak rule.
Vasily Smyslov, the 7th World Champion, is born in Moscow on March 24.
Smyslov dies in Moscow on March 27, two days after his 89th birthday — the longest-lived World Champion.
Mikhail Tal defeats Botvinnik 12½–8½ on March 15 — the ‘Magician from Riga’ becomes the youngest WC at 23.
Ding Liren defeats Ian Nepomniachtchi in the Astana tiebreaks on April 30 — China crowns its first World Champion.
Petrosian and Spassky begin their first WC match on April 9 — ‘Iron Tigran’ holds his title 12½–11½.
Garry Kasparov is born in Baku on April 13 — the future 13th World Champion who would dominate chess for two decades.
Boris Spassky defeats Petrosian on April 14, becoming the 10th World Champion at the second attempt.
Anatoly Karpov is declared World Champion on April 24 after Fischer’s forfeit — a title many felt incomplete until proven over the board.
Anand defends his title against Topalov in Sofia on April 24, winning the last game in dramatic fashion.
Alekhine and Bogoljubov begin their second WC match on April 1 — Alekhine wins comfortably.
Deep Blue defeats Garry Kasparov in their May rematch — the decisive Game 6 on May 11 ends with the World Champion resigning after 19 moves.
Anand and Boris Gelfand begin their WC match in Moscow on May 11 — Anand retains in rapid tiebreaks.
Mikhail Botvinnik, the 6th World Champion and grandfather of Soviet chess, dies in Moscow on May 5 at age 83.
Wilhelm Steinitz, the first official World Champion, is born in Prague on May 14.
Mikhail Botvinnik wins the FIDE match-tournament in Moscow on May 18, becoming the 6th World Champion — the first title decided in a tournament format.
Anatoly Karpov, the 12th World Champion, is born in Zlatoust on May 23.
Final preparations begin for the Match of the Century — Fischer–Spassky negotiations dominate the chess press through late May.
Anatoly Karpov’s birth year approaches — Botvinnik’s WC tournament concludes May 18, opening the Soviet era of chess dominance.
Karpov defeats Gata Kamsky on June 6 in Elista to retain the FIDE WC title — a match held during the schism era.
The first international chess tournament begins in London on May 26, organized by Howard Staunton to coincide with the Great Exhibition.
Tigran Petrosian, the 9th World Champion, is born in Tbilisi on June 17 — ‘Iron Tigran,’ the master of prophylaxis.
Adolf Anderssen plays the ‘Immortal Game’ against Lionel Kieseritzky on June 21 in London — a king’s gambit masterpiece of sacrifice.
Paul Morphy is born in New Orleans on June 22 — the American prodigy who would conquer Europe two decades later.
Mikhail Tal, the 8th World Champion, dies in Moscow on June 28 at age 55 — the romantic genius of attack.
Vera Menchik, the first Women’s World Champion (1927–44), is killed by a V-1 rocket strike on her London home on June 26 — chess loses its undefeated queen.
Vladimir Kramnik, the 14th World Champion, is born in Tuapse on June 25.
Fischer and Spassky open Game 1 of the Match of the Century in Reykjavik on July 11 — chess on the front page of every newspaper, Cold War on the board.
Adolf Anderssen, the strongest player of his era and author of the Immortal Game, is born in Breslau on July 6.
Paul Morphy dies in New Orleans on July 10 at age 47 — the prodigy who had retired from chess decades earlier.
Karpov and Korchnoi begin their WC match in Baguio on July 18 — the famous ‘yogurt’ incident enters chess folklore.
Vera Menchik wins the first Women’s World Championship in London on July 18 — her score of 10½/11 establishes her dominance.
Kasparov and Karpov begin their third match in London/Leningrad on July 28 — Kasparov retains 12½–11½.
Fischer wins Game 6 of the Reykjavik match on July 23 — a positional masterpiece that even Spassky applauds.
Wilhelm Steinitz dies in poverty in Manhattan State Hospital on August 12 — the father of modern chess theory passes in obscurity.
Tigran Petrosian dies in Moscow on August 13 at age 55 — the master of defense leaves a legacy of unbreakable positions.
Mikhail Botvinnik, the 6th World Champion and patriarch of Soviet chess, is born in Kuokkala on August 17.
Lasker and Tarrasch begin their WC match on August 17 — Lasker dominates 10–5 with 5 draws.
Fischer extends his lead at Reykjavik through August with a series of decisive wins — Spassky’s grip on the title slips game by game.
Spassky resigns Game 21 on September 1, conceding the World Championship to Fischer — the first non-Soviet champion since 1948.
Kasparov and Karpov begin their second match in Moscow on September 3 — Kasparov wins 13–11, becoming the 13th and youngest World Champion at 22.
Kasparov defeats Nigel Short in their breakaway PCA match on September 7 — the FIDE schism begins.
Capablanca and Alekhine begin their WC match in Buenos Aires on September 16 — Alekhine wins in a 34-game marathon ending in November.
Kasparov and Anand begin their WC match atop the World Trade Center on September 11 — Kasparov retains 10½–7½.
Kramnik and Topalov begin their reunification match in Elista on September 23 — the ‘Toiletgate’ dispute mars one of chess’s most important matches.
Kramnik defends his classical WC title against Peter Leko in Brissago on September 25 — drawn 7–7, Kramnik retains.
Max Euwe loses his WC title to Alekhine on October 5 — the only Dutch World Champion held the crown for just two years.
Euwe defeats Alekhine on October 3 to become the 5th World Champion — a stunning upset of the alcohol-soaked Russian.
Kramnik defeats Kasparov in London on October 8 — the Berlin Defence demolishes the King’s reign of fifteen years.
Kasparov and Karpov begin their fifth and final WC match in New York/Lyon on October 8 — Kasparov retains 12½–11½.
Alexander Alekhine, the 4th World Champion, is born in Moscow on October 31 — a tactical genius and the only champion to die holding the title.
Donald Byrne and 13-year-old Bobby Fischer play the ‘Game of the Century’ on October 17 in New York — Fischer’s 17...Be6!! launches an immortal queen sacrifice.
Anand defends his title against Kramnik in Bonn on October 14 — Anand wins 6½–4½ to remain undisputed champion.
Lasker and Janowski begin their WC match on October 14 in Paris — a one-sided affair that Lasker wins crushingly.
Paul Morphy plays the ‘Opera Game’ against the Duke of Brunswick and Count Isouard on November 2 in Paris — 17 moves of rapid development still taught to beginners today.
Mikhail Chigorin, father of the Russian school, is born in Gatchina on November 12.
Mikhail Tal, the 8th World Champion, is born in Riga on November 9 — ‘the Magician’ whose sacrifices defied calculation.
Magnus Carlsen and Viswanathan Anand begin their second WC match in Sochi on November 7 — Carlsen retains 6½–4½.
Carlsen and Caruana begin their WC match in London on November 9 — 12 classical draws, Carlsen wins rapid tiebreaks.
José Raúl Capablanca, the 3rd World Champion, is born in Havana on November 19 — the boy who taught himself chess at age four watching his father play.
Carlsen retains his title against Sergey Karjakin in New York on November 30 — the rapid tiebreaks resolve a tied classical match.
Gukesh Dommaraju, age 18, defeats Ding Liren in Singapore on November 25 — the youngest World Champion in chess history.
Magnus Carlsen, the 16th World Champion, is born in Tønsberg on November 30 — the Norwegian prodigy who would hold the title for a decade.
Viswanathan Anand, the 15th World Champion, is born in Mayiladuthurai on December 11 — India’s lightning kid grew up to become a multiple-time champion.
Hikaru Nakamura, future U.S. Champion and rapid world #1, is born in Hirakata on December 9.
Anand defeats Alexei Shirov on December 28 in Tehran to claim the FIDE World Championship — a knockout-format title during the schism.
Emanuel Lasker, the 2nd and longest-reigning World Champion, is born in Berlinchen on December 24.
DeepMind publishes the AlphaZero paper on December 5 — reinforcement-learning chess engine surpasses Stockfish after just hours of self-play.
Steinitz and Isidor Gunsberg play their WC match starting December 9 in New York — Steinitz wins 10½–8½.
Emanuel Lasker dies in New York on January 11 at age 72 — the philosopher-champion who held the title longer than anyone else.
Kasparov’s ‘Pearl of Wijk’ against Topalov on January 20 — a 44-move masterpiece featuring the famous king walk to a4.
The unfinished Kasparov–Karpov match ends in controversy on February 15 — 49 grueling games, no winner declared.
Tigran Petrosian defeats Botvinnik on March 23 in Moscow — the 9th World Champion ends Botvinnik’s long era.
Adolf Anderssen, author of the Immortal and Evergreen games, dies in Breslau on March 13 at age 60.
Alekhine and Bogoljubov begin their first WC match on September 6 — Alekhine wins 15½–9½ in a one-sided contest.
Karpov and Korchnoi begin their second WC match in Merano on October 1 — Karpov dominates 11–7.
Carlsen defeats Nepomniachtchi in Dubai on November 24 — Carlsen’s fifth and final WC title defense before voluntarily relinquishing in 2023.
Magnus Carlsen defeats Anand on November 9 in Chennai to become the 16th World Champion — the Norwegian era begins.
